title: Return Tracks & Send Routing
summary: Return tracks host shared effects (reverb, delay) that multiple sources feed into via send knobs. The correct way to use time-based effects in any mix — everything shares one room.
steps: Create a return track: Cmd+Option+T (Mac) — it appears in the right section of Session view, labelled A, B, C...
Add an effect to the return track (e.g. Reverb, Simple Delay)
Set Dry/Wet to 100% on the return track's effect — the return only outputs the wet effect signal
On any track that should feed this effect, turn up the corresponding Send knob (A, B, etc.) to route signal to the return
The return track's fader controls the overall level of the effect in the mix
Pre vs Post fader send: right-click a Send knob > 'Pre-Fader'. Pre-fader = send level is independent of the track fader. Post-fader (default) = send level scales with the track fader
tips: Always use return tracks for reverb and delay — never as inserts on individual tracks. One shared reverb return = everything exists in the same space. Separate insert reverbs = disconnected, unnatural mix
Name your return tracks clearly: 'Verb LG', 'Verb SM', 'Delay 1/8'. Ableton allows any return track name
High-pass the return track before the reverb/delay: EQ Eight, HP at 200Hz. Removes low-frequency smear from shared effects
For parallel compression (wet/dry compression blend): create a return track with a Compressor at 100% ratio, feed it from the source track, mix back with the fader
ableton_version_note: Return tracks available in all editions of Live 9+.
